summary: DeVault argues that the BDFL (Benevolent Dictator for Life) model is the most effective governance structure for open source projects. He starts from axioms about social systems: that objective rules cannot be programmed into them, and that leadership will ultimately do what they want regardless of formal rules. He critiques Codes of Conduct as attempts to reduce subjective social matters into objective rules, arguing they create exploitable loopholes and paths to power for bad actors. His alternative is simple: leaders should act on their values, delegate to trusted people, and contributors should evaluate whether leadership values align with their own — forking if they don't.
key_points: Social systems are fundamentally different from computer systems, and programmers fall into the trap of thinking objective rules can govern subjective human interactions
The BDFL model is the simplest effective governance because it acknowledges that leadership will ultimately act on their values regardless of formal rules
Codes of Conduct create exploitable loopholes for bad actors and scripted paths to power for people more interested in politics than software
The ability to fork is the escape hatch that makes BDFL governance fair in open source — an option unavailable in sovereign governments
Trust between individuals, not trust in systems, is the foundation of healthy project governance
money_quotes: Objective rules cannot be programmed into a social system.
Project leadership will ultimately do whatever they want, even if they have to come up with an interpretation of the rules which justifies it.
The simplest effective project governance model is a BDFL, which scales a lot further than people might expect.
The relationships in the project are built on trust between individuals, not trust in the system.
Programmers can easily fall into the trap of thinking that objective rules can be applied to social systems, and that they can deal with conflict by executing a script.

Governance models which provide a scripted path onto the decision making committee can often have this path exploited by bad actors, or by people for whom the politics are more important than the software.
Managing drama is a sensitive, subjective issue, but objective rules break hearts.
If someone is being a dickhead, tell them to stop. If they don't stop, kick them out.
key_insight: Open source project governance works best when it's built on trust in people rather than trust in rules, because social systems cannot be reduced to executable scripts.
